simple_definition: The CPU is the brain of the computer that runs programs and controls its functions.
example: When you play a video game on your computer, the CPU processes all the actions and graphics.
analogy: The CPU is like a chef in a kitchen, following recipes to prepare meals (programs) and managing the cooking process (computer tasks).

original_definition: The center of a cluster that is specified by a clustering algorithm, such as k-means or k-median algorithms.
simple_definition: The centroid is the average point that represents the middle of a group of things in a clustering method.
example: In a group of friends who live in different neighborhoods, the centroid would be the point that is closest to everyone's homes on a map.
analogy: The centroid is like the average score of a class; it's the score that best represents everyone's performance.

original_definition: A clustering algorithm that arranges data into non-hierarchical clusters represented by a central vector or a centroid.
simple_definition: A method that groups data into clusters where each group is represented by a central point called a centroid.
example: Grouping customers into segments based on their purchasing behavior, with each segment represented by an average customer profile.
analogy: Like a teacher grouping students based on their test scores, where each group is centered around the average score of the students in that group.

original_definition: A fundamental theorem in calculus that provides a method for computing the derivative of a composite function. Also used in probability to calculate the joint probability of a sequence of events.
simple_definition: The chain rule helps you find the slope of a function made up of other functions.
example: If you want to see how fast the temperature in a room changes when the heater is turned on, you can use the chain rule to combine the effect of the heater and the room's size.
analogy: Think of the chain rule like a series of gears in a machine; when you turn one gear (the outer function), it affects all the other connected gears (the inner functions) in a specific way.

original_definition: A prompt engineering technique that encourages a model to break down complex tasks into smaller steps to show the reasoning process.
simple_definition: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting is a way to ask an AI to explain how it arrives at an answer by breaking down complicated tasks into simpler steps.
example: If asked to solve a math problem like 27 + 15, CoT prompting would have the AI first identify the numbers, then add 20 and 7 separately before combining them to get the final answer.
analogy: It's like following a recipe step-by-step rather than just guessing the final dish.

original_definition: An AI program that utilizes natural language processing techniques to interact with human users through audio or texts conversations.
simple_definition: A chatbot is a computer program that can talk to people using text or speech.
example: A virtual assistant on a company's website that helps customers with their questions.
analogy: A chatbot is like a helpful robot that can answer your questions and have a conversation with you.

original_definition: A practice for saving a snapshot of the variables of a model at a given point in time to allow for exporting model weights and performing training across multiple sessions.
simple_definition: A checkpoint is like a saved game that keeps a record of your progress in training a model so you can come back to it later.
example: Saving your progress in a video game before turning off the console, so you can continue from that point next time you play.
analogy: A checkpoint is like taking a photo of a puzzle after you’ve completed part of it, so you can remember how it looked and continue working on it later.

original_definition: The number of characters in a chunk of text.
simple_definition: Chunk size is how many letters or characters are in a piece of text.
example: If you have a sentence that is 50 characters long, then the chunk size of that sentence is 50.
analogy: Chunk size is like a slice of cake; just as a slice has a certain width, a chunk of text has a specific number of characters.

original_definition: The process of breaking down large amounts of text into smaller more manageable pieces called "chunks".
simple_definition: Chunking means splitting big amounts of text into smaller, easier-to-read parts.
example: When studying for a test, you break the textbook into sections by chapters instead of trying to read it all at once.
analogy: Chunking is like cutting a large pizza into slices so that it's easier to eat.

original_definition: A label that represents one of the enumerated target values.
simple_definition: A class is a category or group to which something belongs.
example: In a school, 'Math' and 'Science' are classes for different subjects.
analogy: A class is like a label on a box that tells you what is inside.

original_definition: A problem in classification when the frequencies of classes in a dataset are significantly different.".
simple_definition: A class-imbalanced dataset is when some categories in a dataset are much more common than others.
example: In a medical study to detect a rare disease, there might be 95 healthy patients for every 5 patients with the disease.
analogy: It's like a classroom where there are 95 students who prefer soccer and only 5 who prefer basketball; the opinions of basketball fans might be overlooked.

original_definition: A type of machine learning algorithms that identify the category to which a specific input belongs.".
simple_definition: Classification is a type of machine learning that figures out what group an item belongs to.
example: An email filter that decides whether a message is spam or not.
analogy: It's like a teacher sorting students into different classes based on their grade levels.

original_definition: A machine learning model that differentiates among two or more discrete classes.
simple_definition: A classification model is a type of computer program that sorts things into different groups.
example: An email filter that separates spam emails from important ones.
analogy: It's like a teacher who groups students based on their reading levels.

original_definition: A value criterion to classify a new observation to binary classification.".
simple_definition: A specific value used to decide if something belongs to one group or another in a two-group classification.
example: In a medical test, a threshold value of 7.0 for blood sugar levels may be used to determine if a person is diabetic or not.
analogy: Think of a basketball free throw line—if a player shoots from behind the line, they score three points; if they shoot from in front of it, they only score two. The line is the classification threshold that decides how many points the shot is worth.

original_definition: An algorithm used for categorizing data into sets (or classes) based on behavioral and structural features.
simple_definition: A classifier is a tool that sorts data into groups based on certain qualities.
example: An email filtering system that sorts emails into 'Spam' and 'Inbox' categories.
analogy: A librarian who organizes books into different sections based on their genres, like fiction or non-fiction.

original_definition: A type of Web analytics that is used to monitor and analyze website visitors.
simple_definition: Clickstream Analytics is a way to track and understand how visitors use a website.
example: An online store uses clickstream analytics to see which products people look at before making a purchase.
analogy: Clickstream Analytics is like a detective following someone's footsteps to see where they've been and what choices they made.

original_definition: A normalization technique used to deal with outliers by capping all feature values above or below a specific value to fixed value.
simple_definition: Clipping is a method to limit numbers by setting a maximum and minimum value, so that extreme values don't affect the results too much.
example: In a dataset of student test scores, if we clip scores above 100 to be 100, then any score that was 105 gets changed to 100.
analogy: Clipping is like having a safety net for tightrope walkers: it stops them from falling too far when they are off balance, just like it keeps values from going too high or too low.

original_definition: A computing model for enabling access to a scalable set of shared computer resources with self-service provision and on-demand administration.
simple_definition: Cloud computing lets you use and store data on the internet instead of on your own computer, and you can access these services whenever you need them.
example: Using Google Drive to store files and access them from any device with internet access.
analogy: Cloud computing is like renting a storage unit: instead of keeping all your things at home, you pay for a space that you can access anytime you want.

original_definition: A comprehensive and integrated environment designed to manage, process, and analyze data in the cloud.
simple_definition: A system that helps you store, handle, and study data using cloud technology.
example: Using Google Drive to save documents and Google Sheets to analyze data allows you to work on projects from anywhere.
analogy: A cloud data ecosystem is like a library in the cloud, where you can borrow books (data), read them (process), and write your own stories (analyze) from any location.

original_definition: A field of robotics in which robots utilize cloud technologies, such as storage and computing, to extend their capabilities.
simple_definition: Cloud robotics is when robots use the internet to get more power and information to do their jobs better.
example: A delivery robot that uses cloud services to navigate and find the best routes to deliver packages.
analogy: Cloud robotics is like a smartphone that uses apps and the internet to perform tasks that would be hard to do alone.

original_definition: A set of objects that are of similar features and organized by similarities.
simple_definition: A group of things that are alike in some way.
example: A cluster of stars in the sky that are close to each other.
analogy: A cluster is like a bunch of grapes; each grape is similar and they grow together on the same vine.

original_definition: A method in unsupervised machine learning for grouping similar objects into clusters.".
simple_definition: Clustering is a way to group similar things together without knowing their categories.
example: Sorting different fruits into groups like apples, bananas, and oranges based on their similarities.
analogy: Clustering is like putting similar colored socks into separate drawers; all the blue socks go in one drawer, the red ones in another, and so on.

original_definition: A problem in neural networks when neurons are highly dependent on other specific neurons instead of the behavior of the network as a whole.
simple_definition: Co-adaptation happens in neural networks when certain neurons rely too much on specific other neurons, instead of working together as a team.
example: In a deep learning model for image recognition, if one neuron is specialized to detect red colors, it may only work well when paired with a neuron that's good at recognizing circles. If those two neurons don't work with others that identify different shapes and colors, the network can be less effective overall.
analogy: It's like a sports team where two players only pass the ball to each other. They become great at that specific play but struggle when they need to work with the whole team to win the game.

original_definition: A semi-supervised learning method used in classification when the unlabeled data is more than labeled data and the dataset has two independent and complementary sets of features.
simple_definition: Co-Training is a method that helps improve classification by using both labeled and unlabeled data when there is more unlabeled data, and it works best with two different sets of features that help describe the data.
example: In email classification, one feature set could be the words in the subject line and the other could be the words in the email body. With a few labeled emails, the algorithm learns to classify them and uses that knowledge to label more unlabeled emails.
analogy: Co-Training is like having two teachers in a classroom. Each teacher focuses on different subjects but helps each other teach the students. Together, they can reach more students than if only one teacher was present.

original_definition: An unsupervised and incremental clustering algorithm that generates a hierarchy of classes.
simple_definition: COBWEB is a method that groups things together based on similarities, creating a tree-like structure of categories.
example: Grouping different types of fruits (like apples, bananas, and oranges) based on their characteristics, such as color and sweetness, to help organize them.
analogy: Think of COBWEB like a family tree, where each branch represents a group of related items, gradually expanding as new members are added.

original_definition: A constant value that is multiplied by a variable or an expression.
simple_definition: A number used to multiply a variable.
example: In the equation 3x, 3 is the coefficient and x is the variable.
analogy: A coefficient is like a recipe's quantity for an ingredient; it tells you how much of something to use.

original_definition: A hypothesis on the fixed structures of a human or artificial mind and the embodied knowledge and skills to produce intelligent behavior.
simple_definition: A theory about the basic parts of a human or computer mind that help it learn and act wisely.
example: A robot designed to play chess, which uses algorithms to evaluate the best moves.
analogy: Cognitive architecture is like the blueprint of a house; it outlines how the house is built and how its rooms function.

original_definition: The use of artificial intelligence technologies to automate tasks that require human cognitive abilities.
simple_definition: Cognitive automation uses AI to do tasks that usually need human thinking.
example: A virtual assistant that can schedule meetings, answer emails, and provide customer support.
analogy: Cognitive automation is like a smartphone with voice recognition that can understand and respond to your commands, making everyday tasks easier.

original_definition: A field of computer science that aims to simulate human thought processes in a computerized model.
simple_definition: Cognitive computing is a part of computer science that tries to make computers think like humans.
example: A virtual assistant like Siri or Alexa that understands and responds to questions in a human-like way.
analogy: Cognitive computing is like training a dog to recognize commands; just as the dog learns to respond to human words and actions, computers learn to understand and respond to human thoughts.

original_definition: A field that focuses on studying the mind and its processes and how they can be simulated in machines.
simple_definition: Cognitive science is the study of how the mind works and how we can make machines think like people.
example: Using computer programs that can recognize speech and respond to questions, like virtual assistants such as Siri or Alexa.
analogy: Cognitive science is like trying to build a robot that can think and learn just like a human brain does.

original_definition: A system that can mimic human cognitive processes such as learning, reasoning, and problem-solving.
simple_definition: A system that can think and learn like a human.
example: A voice assistant that can answer questions and help with tasks.
analogy: Like a smart robot that learns from its experiences to make better decisions.

original_definition: A statistical measure to evaluate the degree of agreement between two raters or to evaluate a classification model performance.
simple_definition: Cohen’s Kappa is a way to see how much two people agree on something, or how well a model makes predictions.
example: In a study, two doctors diagnose whether patients have a certain disease. If both doctors agree on 80 out of 100 patients, Cohen's Kappa can help measure how reliable their agreement is.
analogy: Cohen’s Kappa is like two friends trying to guess the same movie from a trailer. If they often guess the same movie, Kappa shows how much they agree beyond just chance.

original_definition: The storage of inactive data that is only used rarely.
simple_definition: Cold data storage is for data that isn't used often.
example: An online backup service where you store old photos that you only look at once a year.
analogy: It's like putting your winter clothes in the attic until it's cold outside again.

original_definition: AI designed to work alongside humans to achieve shared goals.
simple_definition: AI that helps people work together to reach common goals.
example: AI-powered tools that assist teams in project management by organizing tasks and tracking progress.
analogy: Collaborative AI is like a coach who guides a sports team, helping each player perform better while working towards winning a game together.

original_definition: A technique used in recommendation systems to make predictions about the interests of a user based on the interests of other users.
simple_definition: A method that suggests things to a person by looking at what other people with similar tastes like.
example: If you and your friend both enjoy action movies, and your friend liked a new action movie, you might get a recommendation for that movie too.
analogy: It's like asking a group of friends for book suggestions based on what they've liked, and then picking one based on their favorites.

original_definition: A robot designed for close interaction with a human in a shared workspace.
simple_definition: A robot that works closely with people in the same area.
example: A cobot that helps assembly workers by handing them parts as they build products.
analogy: Like a helpful assistant who stands next to you and hands you tools while you work on a project.

original_definition: The process of finding an optimal solution from a finite set of possibilities when an exhaustive search is not feasible.
simple_definition: Combinatorial optimization is about finding the best solution from a limited number of choices when it's too hard to check them all.
example: A delivery company wants to determine the quickest route to deliver packages to multiple locations while minimizing driving distance.
analogy: Finding the best way to arrange your bookshelf to fit all your books nicely without overcrowding any section is like combinatorial optimization.

original_definition: A model that combines the outputs of multiple models to make a final prediction or decision.
simple_definition: A system that uses the results from several models to come up with one final answer.
example: A weather forecasting system that combines predictions from various meteorologists to give the best forecast.
analogy: Like a group of friends discussing where to eat; everyone gives their opinion, and together they choose the best place to go.

original_definition: The information that helps humans to make sense of everyday activities.
simple_definition: Commonsense knowledge is what people know about the world that helps them understand everyday things.
example: Knowing that you need to wear a coat when it's cold outside.
analogy: Commonsense knowledge is like a user's manual for life; it tells you how to handle everyday situations.

original_definition: The ability of AI systems to understand and make inferences about everyday situations like human commonsense.
simple_definition: Commonsense reasoning is how AI can understand basic everyday things like a human does.
example: If someone sees a wet sidewalk, they can guess it probably rained recently.
analogy: Commonsense reasoning in AI is like a child learning that if they see smoke, there might be a fire.

original_definition: A clustering method that defines the distance between two clusters as the maximum distance between any pair of data points from each cluster.
simple_definition: Complete linkage is a way to measure how far apart two groups of things are by looking at the farthest distance between any two items from each group.
example: Imagine you have two groups of friends. Complete linkage would mean checking the two friends who are the farthest apart in each group and measuring that distance to see how far the groups are from each other.
analogy: It's like finding the longest stretch of road between two cities, where each city represents a group of houses; the longest road controls how far apart the cities are.

original_definition: A flexible approach that enables the assembling and reusing of existing data and analytics components to create customized solutions.
simple_definition: A way to put together and reuse data and analysis tools to make tailored solutions.
example: A company uses pre-made data reports and visualization tools to quickly create a unique sales performance dashboard that meets their specific needs.
analogy: It's like building a Lego structure, where you can use existing Lego pieces in different combinations to create something new each time.

original_definition: An approach that combines different AI techniques to create more advanced systems.
simple_definition: Composite Artificial Intelligence uses different AI methods together to create better systems.
example: A smart personal assistant that uses both natural language processing to understand speech and machine learning to improve responses over time.
analogy: It's like a chef who uses different cooking techniques, like baking and grilling, to make a delicious and unique meal.

original_definition: An emerging field in artificial intelligence that focuses on the ability of machines to both generate and evaluate creative outputs.
simple_definition: Computational Creativity is a new area in AI that looks at how machines can create and judge creative things.
example: An AI program that composes music or writes poetry.
analogy: It's like a robot artist who can both paint pictures and decide which of its own paintings are the best.

original_definition: A branch of artificial intelligence concerned with modeling and generating humor.
simple_definition: Computational humor is a part of AI that tries to create and understand jokes.
example: A chatbot that tells jokes based on what it learns from people, like making puns or funny responses.
analogy: It's like a robot comedian trying to learn how to make people laugh by studying what they find funny.

original_definition: A field that focuses on the biological and linguistical motivation to develop computational approaches, including Neural Networks, Fuzzy Systems, and Evolutionary Computation.
simple_definition: Computational Intelligence is a field that creates computer systems inspired by nature and human thinking, using methods like Neural Networks, Fuzzy Systems, and Evolutionary Computation.
example: A smart assistant like Siri or Alexa that understands spoken language and learns from user interactions.
analogy: Computational Intelligence is like a gardener who observes nature to grow a beautiful garden by nurturing different plants based on their needs.

original_definition: A field of artificial intelligence that focuses on all aspects of designing and analyzing machine learning algorithms to identify learnable problems.".
simple_definition: Computational Learning Theory studies how to create and understand computer programs that can learn from data.
example: A recommendation system like Netflix that learns your movie preferences based on what you've watched.
analogy: It's like teaching a child to recognize animals by showing them pictures and telling them the names, so they learn to identify different animals on their own.

original_definition: A scientific field concerned with understanding human language from a computational perspective.
simple_definition: Computational linguistics is the study of how computers can understand and work with human language.
example: A virtual assistant like Siri or Alexa uses computational linguistics to understand and respond to your spoken commands.
analogy: Computational linguistics is like teaching a robot to understand and speak your language, just as you would teach a child to communicate.

original_definition: A field that focuses on using computational tools and techniques to study the brain and nervous system.
simple_definition: Computational neuroscience uses computer methods to understand how the brain works.
example: Researchers use computer models to simulate how neurons communicate, which can help in understanding diseases like Alzheimer's.
analogy: It's like using a computer simulation to model the weather, helping us predict how storms will form and behave.

original_definition: A field of artificial intelligence that focuses on analyzing and understanding visual data (images and videos).".
simple_definition: Computer vision is a part of AI that helps computers see and understand pictures and videos.
example: Using computer vision, a self-driving car can recognize traffic signs and pedestrians.
analogy: It's like teaching a child to recognize different animals in a book by showing them pictures and explaining what each animal is.

original_definition: A system that assists clinical diagnosis tasks by automated detection in medical records.
simple_definition: A system that helps doctors find problems in medical records automatically.
example: A computer program that analyzes mammograms to identify early signs of breast cancer.
analogy: Like a metal detector helps you find hidden treasures in the sand.

original_definition: An unexpected change that happens over time to the statistical properties of the target variable.
simple_definition: Concept drift refers to changes in what we expect to happen over time when looking at data or trends.
example: For instance, a company's success in selling ice cream may decline during the winter months due to colder weather, which changes customer preferences.
analogy: It's like a football team that plays well in sunny weather but struggles when it rains; the change in weather affects their performance.

original_definition: A probability measure of an event occurring on the condition that another event has already occurred.
simple_definition: Conditional probability is the chance of something happening given that something else has already happened.
example: If it is raining outside, the probability that someone is carrying an umbrella increases. If you know it’s raining, that condition affects the likelihood of the umbrella event.
analogy: It’s like trying to guess how likely someone is to wear a coat when you already know that it’s winter. In winter, the chances of wearing a coat are higher than in summer.

original_definition: A type of interval estimate that contains the range of values required to match the confidence level for estimating the features of observed data.
simple_definition: A confidence interval is a range of values that helps us estimate something about a larger group based on a small sample, showing how sure we are about our estimate.
example: If a survey shows that 60% of people in a town like a new park, a confidence interval might say that between 55% and 65% of all people in the town will like it, giving us a sense of how reliable that 60% figure is.
analogy: Think of a confidence interval like a fishing net: if you cast it wide enough, you are more likely to catch the right fish (the true population value), but if you make it too narrow, you might miss it.

original_definition: The tendency of favoring information that confirms preexisting beliefs or hypotheses.
simple_definition: It's when people only pay attention to information that supports what they already believe.
example: If someone thinks that a certain diet is the best, they may focus only on success stories from that diet and ignore any negative experiences or results from others.
analogy: It's like wearing sunglasses that only let in light of one color; you miss out on the full picture because you're only seeing what you want to see.

original_definition: A matrix that summarizes the performance of a classification model and shows how successful the predictions were.
simple_definition: A table that shows how well a model made predictions by comparing them to the actual results.
example: In a medical test for a disease, a confusion matrix could show how many people were correctly diagnosed as having the disease (true positives), how many healthy people were wrongly diagnosed (false positives), how many sick people were missed (false negatives), and how many were correctly identified as healthy (true negatives).
analogy: Think of a confusion matrix like a report card for a student: it shows not just the grades (predictions) but also how many answers were correct (true positives) and how many mistakes were made (false positives and false negatives).

original_definition: A type of cognitive modeling that uses artificial neural networks to explain intellectual abilities.
simple_definition: Connectionism is a way of understanding how the mind works by using computer programs that act like brain networks.
example: An example of connectionism is using a computer program that learns to recognize faces by analyzing many pictures.
analogy: Connectionism is like a web of connections where each point lights up when it gets information, similar to how our brains react to different experiences.

original_definition: A method to make AI systems more aligned with human values and ethics by creating a set of rules or principles to guide their behavior.
simple_definition: A way to make AI act in ways that match what people believe is right and fair by setting rules for how it should behave.
example: Creating guidelines for an AI used in hiring to ensure it doesn't unfairly favor certain candidates over others.
analogy: Like teaching a child right from wrong by giving them rules to follow, we teach AI how to behave properly in society.

original_definition: A decision problem with finite choices and the objective is to satisfy a given set of constraints by finding values for a set of variables.
simple_definition: A problem where you try to find the right values for some variables so that certain rules are followed.
example: Scheduling classes for students so that no student has two classes at the same time.
analogy: Like fitting puzzle pieces together, where each piece must connect correctly without overlapping or leaving gaps.

original_definition: A machine learning program that generates content by using natural language processing techniques.
simple_definition: A program that creates text using smart language understanding.
example: A chatbot that writes stories or answers questions automatically.
analogy: Like a digital writer that can create stories and articles on its own.

original_definition: The practice of monitoring and applying predefined rules and guidelines to the users' submissions to determine the content's appropriateness.
simple_definition: Content moderation is checking what people post online to make sure it's okay to share.
example: Social media platforms like Facebook review posts and comments to remove anything that’s offensive or breaks their rules.
analogy: Content moderation is like a librarian who makes sure all the books in a library are suitable for readers and removes any that are inappropriate.

original_definition: A technique used in recommendation systems to make predictions about a user's interests based on the item's features and user's likes.
simple_definition: Content-Based Filtering is a way to suggest things to users based on what they like and the features of those items.
example: If you enjoy action movies, a streaming service might recommend other movies that are also in the action genre.
analogy: It's like a chef who remembers your favorite ingredients and uses them to create dishes that you would likely enjoy.

original_definition: The maximum number of tokens a language model can process and remember at one time.
simple_definition: The most words a language model can understand and remember at once.
example: If a language model has a context length of 100 tokens, it can only keep track of the last 100 words from the conversation.
analogy: It's like a bucket that can only hold a certain amount of water; once it's full, you can't add more without spilling some out.

original_definition: A word representation that captures the meaning of words based on their context within a text.
simple_definition: A way to represent words that shows what they mean based on the words around them.
example: In the sentence 'The bank can be a place to store money' and 'The bank of the river was beautiful', the word 'bank' has different meanings based on the context.
analogy: Just like how the meaning of 'bark' changes depending on whether we are talking about a tree or a dog, contextual embeddings change the meaning of words based on their surroundings.

original_definition: A feature that has an infinite number of possible values.
simple_definition: A feature that can take on any value within a range.
example: The height of a person, which can be 5.5 feet, 5.75 feet, etc.
analogy: Like a water hose that can adjust its flow rate to any level, rather than just having it fully on or off.

original_definition: A neural network model that learns to associate images with their corresponding text descriptions by training on a massive dataset of image-text pairs.
simple_definition: CLIP is a computer program that learns to match pictures with words by studying many examples of images and their descriptions.
example: If you show CLIP a picture of a dog and say 'dog,' it learns to connect the word 'dog' with images of dogs.
analogy: CLIP is like a student who learns to link pictures and words by looking at a big book of flashcards that show various objects and their names.

original_definition: A machine learning technique that learns meaningful representations by contrasting positive and negative examples.
simple_definition: Contrastive learning is a way for computers to understand and learn from examples by comparing things that are similar and different.
example: Imagine teaching a computer to recognize cats and dogs by showing it pictures of both. You show it images of cats (positive examples) and images of dogs (negative examples) so it can learn the differences.
analogy: It's like a student who learns to identify different fruits by comparing apples and oranges. The more they see the differences and similarities, the better they get at recognizing each fruit.

original_definition: A loss function that encourages the model to learn embeddings by minimizing the distance between similar pairs of data points and maximizing the distance between dissimilar pairs.
simple_definition: Contrastive loss helps a model learn by making similar items closer together and different items farther apart.
example: In facial recognition, if two photos are of the same person, the model should make their embeddings closer. If they are of different people, the model should push their embeddings apart.
analogy: It's like a teacher grading students: if two students answer questions similarly, the teacher gives them a higher similarity score, while giving lower scores to students with very different answers.

original_definition: A field of applied mathematics that deals with dynamical systems' behavior by using feedback to influence the behavior and achieve the desired goal.
simple_definition: Control Theory is the study of how to make systems behave in a certain way using feedback.
example: A thermostat in a house uses control theory to keep the temperature at a set level by turning the heating on or off.
analogy: Control Theory is like a coach guiding a sports team; the coach gives feedback to help the players improve and reach their goal of winning the game.

original_definition: The property of an AI system in which external entities can intervene in its functioning.
simple_definition: Controllability means that people can influence how an AI system works.
example: A smart thermostat can be adjusted by the user to change the temperature settings.
analogy: Controllability is like a remote control for a TV, allowing you to change channels or adjust the volume.

original_definition: A method for collecting data from easily accessible sources.
simple_definition: It's a way to collect information from people who are easy to reach.
example: A teacher surveys students in her own classroom instead of reaching out to students from other schools.
analogy: It's like picking the ripest fruit from the nearest tree instead of searching the entire orchard.

original_definition: A state reached by a model when additional training on the existing data will not significantly improve the model.
simple_definition: Convergence is when a model has learned as much as it can from the data, and more training won't help it get better.
example: A student studying for a test knows all the material well and can't improve their score by studying more.
analogy: It's like a car that has reached its maximum speed; no matter how much you push the pedal, it won't go any faster.

original_definition: The technologies that use machine learning and natural language processing to provide human-like interaction with users via text or voice.
simple_definition: Conversational Artificial Intelligence is technology that allows computers to talk or text with people in a way that feels natural, using learned patterns of language.
example: A virtual assistant like Siri or Google Assistant that can answer questions and perform tasks through voice commands.
analogy: Conversational Artificial Intelligence is like a helpful librarian who knows how to talk and find information you need, whether you ask in person or by messaging.

original_definition: A software solution that enables the development and deployment of AI-powered conversational interfaces for natural language interactions between humans and computers.
simple_definition: A tool that helps create and use chatbots and voice assistants to talk with people using natural language.
example: Siri on your iPhone or Alexa in your home are examples of conversational AI platforms.
analogy: A conversational AI platform is like a bridge that connects humans and computers, allowing them to communicate easily.

original_definition: A user interface that allows users to interact with computers in a way similar to a human-to-human conversation.
simple_definition: A way for users to talk to computers like they would talk to another person.
example: Using a virtual assistant like Siri or Alexa to set reminders or ask for weather updates.
analogy: It's like having a chat with a helpful friend who knows a lot about technology.

original_definition: A function in which the area above its graph is a convex set.
simple_definition: A convex function is one where the line connecting any two points on the graph lies above or on the graph itself.
example: The function f(x) = x^2 is a convex function because if you pick any two points on its curve and draw a straight line between them, the line will always be above the curve.
analogy: Imagine a bowl: if you take any two points on the edge of the bowl and connect them with a straight string, the string will always be above the surface of the bowl.

original_definition: A process of finding the minimum of a convex function using mathematical techniques.
simple_definition: Convex optimization is about finding the lowest point of a curved surface, where the surface always curves upwards.
example: A company wants to minimize its costs while producing a product, so it looks for the best combination of materials and labor to achieve that.
analogy: Finding the lowest point in a bowl of water; no matter where you drop a marble, it will roll down to the bottom.

original_definition: A subset of Euclidean space in which a drawn line between any two points in the subset stays totally within the subset.
simple_definition: A convex set is a group of points where if you connect any two points with a straight line, the entire line stays inside the group.
example: A solid circle or a filled square is a convex set because any line between two points inside stays inside.
analogy: Think of a convex set like a rubber band stretched in a circle; if you pick any two points on it, the line between them will always be inside the circle.

original_definition: A process of filtering to train weights by mixing the convolutional filter and the input matrix.
simple_definition: Convolution is a way to apply a filter to an input to help a computer learn important patterns.
example: Using a convolutional filter to identify the edges in an image, like finding outlines of objects in a photo.
analogy: Think of convolution like using a stencil: when you place a stencil over a piece of paper and paint over it, the paint only reaches certain areas, revealing a pattern.

original_definition: A filter that performs convolution operations by scanning the input while considering its dimensions.".
simple_definition: A convolutional filter is a tool that looks at different parts of an input, like a picture, to find patterns.
example: In image processing, a convolutional filter can be used to identify edges in an image by scanning it with a specific pattern.
analogy: It's like using a stencil to paint designs on a surface; the stencil filters out everything except for the shape you want to see.

original_definition: A layer in a convolutional neural network that uses filters to perform convolution operations.
simple_definition: A part of a neural network that uses small windows to look at parts of the data and pick out important features.
example: In image recognition, a convolutional layer helps identify shapes or patterns in photos, like edges or textures.
analogy: It's like using a magnifying glass to focus on small areas of a painting to see the details more clearly.

original_definition: A type of deep neural network used to create models for analyzing and processing images and videos.
simple_definition: A special kind of computer program that helps understand and analyze pictures and videos.
example: Using CNNs to recognize faces in photos on social media.
analogy: Like a chef who learns to identify different ingredients in a dish by tasting it many times, a CNN learns to recognize patterns in images by processing lots of them.

original_definition: A mathematical operation of multiplying a slice of an input matrix and the convolutional filter and then summing all the values in the resulting matrix.
simple_definition: Convolution is a way to combine a small part of an image with a filter to find patterns, by multiplying and adding the numbers together.
example: In image processing, a convolutional operation helps to detect edges in a photo by using a filter designed to highlight changes in brightness.
analogy: Think of convolution like using a stencil to paint a design. You press the stencil on a surface, apply paint, and where the stencil is, the paint colors the specific area, creating a pattern.

original_definition: The step size of shifting the convolution filters.
simple_definition: The distance the filter moves when applying it to an image.
example: When processing a picture, if the filter moves 2 pixels to the right each time, that means it has a stride of 2.
analogy: It's like walking down a hallway: if you take big steps, you get to the end faster, but if you take tiny steps, you notice more of the details along the way.

original_definition: A task in natural language processing (NLP) that involves identifying all expressions in a text that refer to the same entity.
simple_definition: Coreference resolution is figuring out which words in a text refer to the same person, place, or thing.
example: In the sentence 'Alice loves her dog. She takes it for walks every day.', 'She' and 'it' refer to Alice and her dog, respectively.
analogy: It’s like solving a puzzle where you connect different pieces that show the same picture.

original_definition: A large and structured collection of text or audio data.
simple_definition: A big and organized set of written or spoken material.
example: A collection of all the books in a library.
analogy: Like a museum that stores many different paintings for people to study.

original_definition: A statistical measure of the extent to which two variables are mathematically related.
simple_definition: Correlation is a way to see how closely two things are related to each other.
example: As the temperature rises in summer, ice cream sales often increase, showing a positive correlation between temperature and ice cream sales.
analogy: Correlation is like the relationship between studying and test scores; when you study more, your test scores tend to improve.

original_definition: A method used to explain the decisions made by machine learning models by describing what changes to the input data would have resulted in a different outcome.
simple_definition: Counterfactual explanation shows how changing the input data could lead to a different result from a machine learning model.
example: If a loan application was rejected, a counterfactual explanation might say that if the applicant's income was $10,000 higher, the loan would have been approved.
analogy: It's like asking, 'What if I had taken a different route to work?' If I had chosen a different road, I might have avoided traffic and arrived on time.

original_definition: A fairness metric that evaluates whether a classifier makes the same result for identical individuals except for one or more sensitive attributes.
simple_definition: Counterfactual fairness checks if a machine learning model gives the same result for two people who are otherwise identical, but differ in sensitive traits like race or gender.
example: If a job application system denies a qualified woman the job only because she is a woman, while a man with the same qualifications would have been accepted, that shows a lack of counterfactual fairness.
analogy: It's like a teacher giving a student a lower grade just because they are from a certain neighborhood, even though the student's work is identical to that of a classmate from a different neighborhood.

original_definition: A measure of the variability of two unrelated variables.
simple_definition: Covariance shows how two different things change together.
example: If you look at the changes in temperature and ice cream sales, higher temperatures could lead to more ice cream sales, showing positive covariance.
analogy: Covariance is like seeing how two friends behave together; if one gets excited and the other does too, they have a positive covariance in their energy levels.

original_definition: A type of selection bias in which the selected data does not represent the target population.
simple_definition: Coverage bias happens when the data we collect doesn't accurately reflect the group we're studying.
example: If a survey about smartphone usage only includes people from a tech convention, it may miss the habits of older adults who don't attend such events.
analogy: It's like trying to understand what everyone in a city likes to eat by only asking people at a vegan restaurant.

original_definition: A mechanism for evaluating a model generalization on new data by testing the model against one or more subsets taken from the training set.
simple_definition: Cross-validation is a way to check how well a model works on new data by using different parts of the training data to test it.
example: If you're training a model to predict house prices, you might use part of the data to train the model and another part to see how well it predicts prices.
analogy: Cross-validation is like a teacher giving students different quizzes on different chapters to see how well they understand the material overall, rather than just one big test.

original_definition: A machine learning training strategy where models are trained on data in a meaningful sequence, starting with easier examples and gradually progressing to more difficult ones.
simple_definition: Curriculum learning is a way to train machines by starting with simple tasks and slowly moving to harder ones.
example: Teaching a child to read by first introducing them to letters, then simple words, and finally complete sentences.
analogy: It's like climbing a staircase, where you start on the lower steps and gradually move up to the higher ones.

original_definition: An estimator that is designed to follow specific directions.
simple_definition: A custom estimator is a tool made to do a specific task based on particular instructions.
example: A custom estimator could be a calculator program that only performs addition and subtraction according to the user's requirements.
analogy: It's like a tailor who makes a suit to fit a person's exact measurements instead of using a standard size.

original_definition: A model that accepts inputs specific to a user and returns predictions based on those inputs.
simple_definition: A custom model is a type of program that takes specific information from a user and gives predictions based on that information.
example: A custom model for recommending movies might take your favorite genres and recent movies you've watched to suggest new films you might like.
analogy: A custom model is like a personal shopper who asks about your tastes and preferences to find the perfect clothes for you.

original_definition: The process that trains a model to make particular predictions.
simple_definition: Custom training is when we teach a model to make specific predictions.
example: Training a model to recognize different dog breeds based on pictures.
analogy: It's like coaching a soccer player to improve their skills in scoring goals; you focus on what they need to learn to succeed.

original_definition: The data that organizations collect, process, and store during regular business activities but do not use for other purposes.
simple_definition: Dark data is information that businesses gather but don't use for anything useful.
example: A retail store collects customer purchase receipts but never analyzes them to understand buying trends.
analogy: Dark data is like leftovers in your fridge that you never eat; they take up space but could have been turned into a delicious meal.

original_definition: A graphical user interface that combines information and visualizations to view metrics, benchmarks, or indicators that assist in monitoring and decision-making.
simple_definition: A dashboard is a visual display that shows important information and data in one place to help people make decisions.
example: A car's dashboard shows the speed, fuel level, and engine temperature to help the driver understand how the car is performing.
analogy: A dashboard is like an airplane cockpit where pilots can quickly see important information about the flight to make sure everything is running smoothly.

original_definition: The representation of information in a suitable format for storage, processing, or transmission.
simple_definition: Data is information that is organized so it can be saved, used, or shared.
example: A list of names and phone numbers saved in your contacts on a smartphone.
analogy: Data is like ingredients in a recipe; when organized properly, they can create a delicious dish.

original_definition: The process of data gathering from several sources for analysis or reporting, including data grouping or summarization, such as sum, average, minimum, or maximum.
simple_definition: Data aggregation is collecting and summarizing data from different places to analyze or report it.
example: A school collects test scores from all classes to calculate the average score for the entire school.
analogy: Data aggregation is like making a smoothie; you gather different fruits, blend them together, and end up with a tasty drink that represents all the fruits.

original_definition: A systemic examination of data through samples, measurement, and visualization.
simple_definition: Data analysis is the process of looking at data carefully to find patterns or insights.
example: A store analyzes customer purchase data to see which products are the most popular.
analogy: Data analysis is like a detective examining clues to solve a mystery.

original_definition: A concept that consists of collecting, validating, processing, and visualizing data to obtain insights useful for decision-making.
simple_definition: Data analytics is looking at data to understand it better and make good choices.
example: A restaurant analyzes its sales data to find out which dishes are the most popular and which times are busiest, helping them decide on menu changes and staffing.
analogy: Data analytics is like a detective looking at clues to solve a mystery; by gathering all the information, they can find out what happened and make informed decisions.

original_definition: The process of adding descriptive information to the dataset to be used as an input for a machine learning model.".
simple_definition: Data annotation is when you add labels or comments to a dataset to help a computer learn from it.
example: Labeling pictures of animals where each image is tagged with the correct animal name, like 'cat' or 'dog'.
analogy: It's like putting name tags on items at a yard sale so that buyers know what each item is and how much it costs.

original_definition: The process of removing or modifying personally identifiable information from datasets to protect individuals' privacy.
simple_definition: Data anonymization means changing personal information in data so that people can't be identified.
example: When a hospital shares patient data for research, they remove names and addresses so patients' identities are kept private.
analogy: Data anonymization is like blurring faces in a photo to protect people's identities.

original_definition: A machine learning technique to create new data by manipulating the original data.
simple_definition: Data augmentation is a way to make more training data by changing the original data a little.
example: If you have a set of images of cats, you can create new images by rotating, flipping, or changing the colors of those original images.
analogy: Data augmentation is like making multiple copies of a painting and then slightly changing each one to create a unique collection.

original_definition: A physical facility that houses many servers and data storage devices with high-speed connectivity to manage the organization's applications and data.
simple_definition: A data center is a building where lots of computers and storage devices are kept to handle an organization's data and applications quickly.
example: Amazon Web Services (AWS) operates data centers that store and manage data for businesses and websites.
analogy: A data center is like a library, but instead of books, it stores data on computers and helps organizations find and use that data efficiently.

original_definition: The process of assigning categories or labels to data based on its content, sensitivity, or other criteria to improve organization, security, and compliance.
simple_definition: Data classification is when we put data into groups based on what it is, how sensitive it is, or other factors to make it easier to manage and protect.
example: A school classifies students' information by categories such as personal data, academic performance, and health records to ensure privacy and proper handling.
analogy: Data classification is like sorting your books into different shelves based on their genres, such as fiction, non-fiction, and reference, so you can easily find what you need.

original_definition: The process of correcting errors, inconsistencies, and inaccuracies in datasets.".
simple_definition: Data cleansing is fixing mistakes and problems in data.
example: If a spreadsheet of customer names has one entry that says 'Jonh' instead of 'John', data cleansing would correct that spelling error.
analogy: Data cleansing is like proofreading a book to fix typos and grammar mistakes.

original_definition: The process of gathering and measuring data, including data acquisition, data labeling, and data improvement.
simple_definition: Data collection is the way we gather and organize information.
example: A school surveys students to find out their favorite subjects.
analogy: Data collection is like picking apples from a tree; you gather fruit (data) to find out which ones are the best (most useful).

original_definition: The policies, procedures, and tools used to manage and protect data throughout its lifecycle.
simple_definition: Data control means the rules and tools we use to keep data safe and organized from start to finish.
example: A bank uses data control to protect customer information, making sure only authorized employees can access it and that it is secure from theft.
analogy: Data control is like a library system, where books are cataloged, and only library members can check them out, ensuring they are tracked and kept safe.

original_definition: A person or organization in charge of providing IT infrastructure and protecting data in conformance to data governance policies and practices.
simple_definition: A data custodian is someone or a group that manages and protects data using rules for handling that data.
example: A company's IT department that stores customer information safely and ensures it follows security policies.
analogy: A data custodian is like a librarian who organizes and safeguards books, making sure they are used correctly and kept in good condition.

original_definition: A statistical bias that occurs when data is manipulated or analyzed excessively to produce statistically significant results that may not be truly significant.".
simple_definition: Data dredging is when data is over-analyzed in order to find results that seem important, but actually aren't real or meaningful.
example: A researcher looks at a large dataset of people's eating habits and finds that those who eat pizza often have a high income. However, this connection might be coincidental and not something that really stands up to further testing.
analogy: It's like searching through a big box of mixed-up toys trying to find a rare one, but instead of looking for one specific toy, you keep taking out random toys hoping to find something valuable, even if you end up just wasting time and not actually finding anything special.

original_definition: A change that happens over time in the distribution of model input data, which can cause a decline in model performance.".
simple_definition: Data drift is when the kind of information we feed into a model changes over time, which can make the model work not as well.
example: A company that uses a model to predict customer purchasing habits finds that, after a few months, customers are now buying more online instead of in stores, which affects the model's accuracy.
analogy: Data drift is like a plant that needs specific soil to grow; if the type of soil changes over time, the plant may not thrive as it used to.

original_definition: A field that focuses on designing, building, and maintaining the infrastructure and systems needed to support the collection, processing, storage, and analysis of large volumes of data.
simple_definition: Data engineering is about creating and managing the tools and systems that help gather, process, store, and analyze a lot of data.
example: A company like Netflix uses data engineering to handle user data so they can recommend shows and movies based on what you like.
analogy: Data engineering is like building a highway system that allows cars (data) to move smoothly from one place (source) to another (destination) without getting stuck in traffic (bottlenecks).

original_definition: The principles and standards that govern the collection, processing, sharing, and use of data.
simple_definition: Data ethics are the rules that tell us how to properly collect, use, and share data responsibly.
example: A company that collects personal information from users must ensure it protects that information and doesn't share it without consent.
analogy: Data ethics is like following traffic rules; just as drivers must obey signals and signs to keep everyone safe on the road, organizations must follow data ethics to protect people's privacy and data.

original_definition: Any information produced as a byproduct of digital activities.
simple_definition: Data exhaust is the extra information created when we use digital tools and services.
example: When you browse the internet, the websites you visit and the searches you make create data exhaust.
analogy: Data exhaust is like the crumbs left behind when you eat a snack; they may not be the main meal, but they still tell you something about what you consumed.

original_definition: An architecture for data management and integration that provides consistent capabilities across multiple environments.
simple_definition: Data Fabric is a system that helps manage and connect data from different places in a consistent way.
example: A company uses Data Fabric to combine customer information from its online store, physical stores, and customer support into one view so employees can easily access all the data.
analogy: Data Fabric is like a universal remote control that can operate different devices in your home, allowing you to manage everything from one place.

original_definition: A way to provide users with updated data from data sources.
simple_definition: A way to send users new information from data sources regularly.
example: A news app that updates users with the latest articles and headlines.
analogy: Like a subscription service that delivers fresh newspapers to your door every morning.

original_definition: The process of combining data from different sources to provide more consistent and accurate information.
simple_definition: Data fusion is mixing information from different places to get better and more accurate details.
example: A weather app combines data from satellites, weather stations, and radar to give you a complete forecast.
analogy: Data fusion is like making a vegetable soup by combining carrots, potatoes, and onions to create a more flavorful dish.

original_definition: The policies, processes, and organizational structures that define the decision rights and accountabilities to support data management.
simple_definition: Data governance is the rules and structures that help manage and protect data within an organization.
example: A company creates a set of rules about who can access customer information and how it should be stored. This ensures that only authorized people can see sensitive data and that it's kept safe.
analogy: Data governance is like the rules of a library: it defines who can check out books, how they should be returned, and how to keep the collection organized and secure.

original_definition: The process of combining disparate data into a consistent format to facilitate their access and analysis.
simple_definition: Data harmonization is mixing different types of data into a uniform format so it's easier to use and understand.
example: A hospital combines patient records from various departments, such as surgery and outpatient care, into a single system that allows doctors to see a complete medical history.
analogy: Data harmonization is like tuning a bunch of musical instruments to the same pitch so they can all play together in harmony.

original_definition: A central repository for collecting, storing, managing, and sharing data collected from multiple sources.
simple_definition: A data hub is a main place where data from different sources is kept, organized, and shared.
example: A city's traffic management system that collects data from various traffic cameras and sensors to monitor and manage traffic flow.
analogy: A data hub is like a library where books from different authors are gathered in one place for everyone to access and read.

original_definition: The process of maintaining accurate, complete, and consistent data within a system or database.
simple_definition: Data hygiene means keeping data clean and correct so that it works well.
example: A company regularly checks and fixes its customer information to make sure addresses and phone numbers are up-to-date.
analogy: Data hygiene is like brushing your teeth; just as you clean your teeth to avoid cavities and maintain health, you clean your data to avoid mistakes and keep it healthy.

original_definition: A selection of a data item from a datatype or a dataset.
simple_definition: A single piece of data taken from a collection.
example: A student's grade from a list of grades in a class.
analogy: Like picking one apple from a basket full of apples.

original_definition: The process of combining data residing in different sources into a meaningful unified view to users.
simple_definition: Data integration is when you bring together information from different places to make it easier to understand all at once.
example: A company combines customer data from its website, social media, and sales records to see a complete picture of customer preferences.
analogy: Data integration is like making a fruit salad; you chop up different fruits and mix them together to create a tasty dish that showcases all the flavors.

original_definition: The trust measure for data accuracy, completeness, consistency, and compliance with regulations.
simple_definition: Data integrity means that the information is correct, complete, and reliable according to the rules.
example: A hospital uses patient records that are regularly checked to ensure the details are accurate and up to date, which helps avoid medical errors.
analogy: Data integrity is like keeping your library organized; if every book is in the right place and has the right information, anyone can find what they need without confusion.

original_definition: The use of AI technologies to analyze and interpret data.
simple_definition: Using AI to understand and make sense of data.
example: A company uses AI to analyze customer buying patterns to improve their marketing strategies.
analogy: Data intelligence is like a detective using clues to solve a mystery.

original_definition: A repository for storing a large amount of raw data in different formats.
simple_definition: A data lake is a place where you can keep lots of different types of raw data together.
example: An e-commerce company collects data from customer purchases, website visits, and social media interactions, storing all this information in a data lake.
analogy: A data lake is like a large filing cabinet where you can shove in all sorts of documents without any organization, waiting for someone to pull them out and sort through them later.

original_definition: A data architecture that combines the best features of a data lake and data warehouse into a single platform.
simple_definition: A data lakehouse is a storage system that mixes the strengths of both a data lake and a data warehouse in one place.
example: A company uses a data lakehouse to store raw data from social media, sales, and customer feedback, allowing both data scientists and analysts to access and analyze it efficiently.
analogy: A data lakehouse is like a versatile tool that combines a hammer and a screwdriver, allowing you to handle different types of tasks with just one tool.

original_definition: A situation in machine learning where information from outside the training dataset is used to create the model, leading to overly optimistic performance estimates.
simple_definition: Data leakage happens when extra information that shouldn’t be part of the training data is used, making the model seem better than it really is.
example: If a medical model uses patient information from future test results to predict past diagnoses, it will perform well in tests but won’t work in real situations.
analogy: It's like a student who looks at the answer key while studying for a test; they might think they know the answers, but they won't do well when the answers aren’t available.

original_definition: The ability to understand, analyze, and communicate data effectively, making informed decisions based on data-driven insights.
simple_definition: Data literacy means being able to read, understand, and use data to make smart choices.
example: A teacher uses test scores to see which subjects students are struggling with and then decides to spend more time on those subjects.
analogy: Data literacy is like being able to read a recipe. Just as understanding the ingredients and steps helps you cook a meal, understanding data helps you make better decisions.

original_definition: The process of importing, transferring, or integrating data from various sources into a single storage system, such as a database or data warehouse.
simple_definition: Data loading means bringing data from different places into one storage area, like a database.
example: When a company collects customer information from online forms, sales records, and social media, and combines it into one database for analysis.
analogy: Data loading is like collecting ingredients from different stores to make a meal; you gather everything you need into one kitchen before cooking.

original_definition: An electronic platform that primarily provides data or any related services and allows the buying and selling of machine-readable data.
simple_definition: A website where people can buy and sell data that computers can read.
example: Amazon Web Services Data Exchange, where users can purchase datasets on various topics.
analogy: A farmer's market where vendors sell fresh produce and customers can choose what they want to buy.

original_definition: A data warehouse subset that focuses on a specific line of business or subject area.
simple_definition: A data mart is a smaller part of a data warehouse that focuses on a specific business area.
example: A sales data mart that contains information only about sales transactions and customer purchases.
analogy: A data mart is like a specific section in a bookstore that only sells mystery novels, while the entire bookstore represents the data warehouse.

original_definition: The process of obscuring sensitive information by replacing it with fictitious, yet realistic-looking data.".
simple_definition: Data masking is hiding important information by changing it to fake, but believable data.
example: Instead of showing a real credit card number, a bank might show a masked version like 'XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-1234'.
analogy: Data masking is like wearing a disguise; you look different on the outside but still represent someone recognizable.

original_definition: The process of transferring data from one system to another.
simple_definition: Data migration is moving information from one place to another.
example: When a company upgrades to a new software system and moves all its customer records to the new system.
analogy: Data migration is like packing your belongings and moving them from one house to another.

original_definition: The process of discovering patterns from a large amount of data and extracting useful information.
simple_definition: Data mining is finding useful information from a lot of data.
example: A store analyzes customer purchase data to understand buying trends and improve sales.
analogy: Data mining is like panning for gold in a river; you sift through a lot of sand to find valuable nuggets.

original_definition: A group of conceptual methods for describing data, data semantics, data relationships, and consistency constraints.
simple_definition: A way to organize and explain data, how it relates to each other, and rules for keeping it accurate.
example: A library system that tracks books, authors, and borrowers, showing how they are connected.
analogy: A family tree that shows how family members are related to each other and the rules of the family.

original_definition: The process of building a conceptual representation of data and their relationships to be stored in a database.
simple_definition: Data modeling is making a simple map of how data is organized and related in a database.
example: Creating a model for a library database that shows how books, authors, and borrowers are connected.
analogy: Data modeling is like drawing a blueprint for a house, where different rooms represent different types of data and how they connect to each other.

original_definition: The ability to monitor, understand, and maintain the health of data to ensure its accuracy, consistency, and reliability.
simple_definition: Data observability means being able to check and keep track of data to make sure it's correct and trustworthy.
example: A company uses data observability tools to monitor their sales data. If they notice a sudden drop in sales figures, they can quickly investigate and find out that there was an issue with how the data was collected.
analogy: Data observability is like a car's dashboard, which shows the driver important information about the car's performance, such as fuel level and engine temperature. Just like the dashboard helps keep the car running smoothly, data observability helps ensure that data remains accurate and reliable.

original_definition: A data management practice that uses agile and collaborative methodology to streamline the lifecycle of data analytics and improve data quality.
simple_definition: DataOps is a way to manage data that makes it easier and faster to analyze by working together and improving data quality.
example: A company uses DataOps to quickly gather and analyze customer feedback from various sources, improving their product based on real-time data insights.
analogy: DataOps is like a well-coordinated team in a kitchen, where chefs and servers work together efficiently to deliver high-quality meals quickly.

original_definition: An individual or people who are accountable for particular data.
simple_definition: A data owner is someone responsible for specific information.
example: A teacher who keeps student grades is a data owner of those grades.
analogy: A data owner is like a gardener who takes care of a specific patch of plants; they manage and are responsible for their health.

original_definition: A technique used to speed up training or inference by duplicating the model on multiple devices and then splitting up the input data over these devices.
simple_definition: Data parallelism is a way to make training or running a model faster by using many devices that each work on a part of the input data.
example: Imagine a team of chefs in a restaurant, where each chef prepares different dishes at the same time using the same recipe.
analogy: Data parallelism is like a relay race, where each runner (device) carries a portion of the baton (data) and passes it to the next runner to finish the race faster.

original_definition: A series of processes that move and transform raw data from various sources to a destination for analysis or use.
simple_definition: A data pipeline is a way to take raw data from different places, process it, and send it somewhere to be used or analyzed.
example: A company collects sales data from their website, processes that data to understand trends, and then sends it to their analytics tool for reporting.
analogy: A data pipeline is like a water pipeline that takes water from a reservoir, filters it, and delivers it to your home for drinking.

original_definition: A discrete piece of information derived from a larger dataset.
simple_definition: A single piece of information from a larger group of data.
example: The temperature reading of a city on a particular day.
analogy: A data point is like a single bead in a necklace, where each bead represents one piece of information in a long chain.

original_definition: A security attack where an attacker injects false or misleading data into the training dataset to corrupt or influence the model's behavior.
simple_definition: Data poisoning is when someone adds bad data to a learning system so it learns the wrong things.
example: A spammer might add fake emails to a system that filters out spam, tricking the filter into allowing their unwanted messages through.
analogy: It's like a chef getting bad ingredients for a recipe; if the chef uses spoiled items, the final dish will taste terrible.

original_definition: A wide range of operations that can be carried out on data by manual or automated means.
simple_definition: Data processing is the way we handle and work with data, either by hand or using machines.
example: Sorting a list of names alphabetically using a computer program.
analogy: Data processing is like cooking: you gather ingredients (data), follow a recipe (instructions), and create a dish (output) by combining everything together.

original_definition: The process of examining the data and creating informative summaries about it.
simple_definition: Data profiling is checking data to understand what it looks like and summarize its details.
example: A company analyzes its customer database to find out how many customers are in each age group, what countries they come from, and how many purchases they've made.
analogy: Data profiling is like a doctor checking a patient’s vital signs and health history to get a complete picture of their health.

original_definition: Data that substitutes for missing, inaccessible, or immeasurable data by using related existing data from a similar process or activity.
simple_definition: Data that stands in for data we can't find or measure by using similar data from another situation.
example: If we can't get temperature data from a specific city, we might use temperature data from a nearby city that has similar weather.
analogy: Using a substitute teacher when the regular teacher is absent; the substitute teaches based on the same curriculum.

original_definition: A measure of data usefulness and fit for purpose, including completeness, consistency, and accuracy.
simple_definition: Data quality is how good and reliable data is, including if it's complete, consistent, and correct.
example: A customer database that has all necessary information like names, addresses, and purchase history without any mistakes is an example of good data quality.
analogy: Data quality is like a recipe; if you have all the right ingredients in the right amounts, you'll make a great dish, but if something is missing or wrong, it won't turn out well.

original_definition: The process of selectively removing or obscuring sensitive information from a document or dataset to protect it from unauthorized access.
simple_definition: Data redaction means hiding important information in a document so that only safe parts can be seen.
example: A hospital removes patients' names and personal details from medical records before sharing them for research.
analogy: Data redaction is like blacking out sensitive words in a top-secret letter so that only the safe information can be read.

original_definition: The duplication of data on several computers within a distributed system to ensure the consistency level for accessing information.
simple_definition: Data replication means making copies of data on different computers to keep the information the same everywhere.
